Google Is Reportedly A Major Intel Foundry Customer, Will Use EMIB Advanced Packaging For Next-Gen TPU. Google's forthcoming TPUv8e AI chip is rumored to be manufactured by Intel Foundry, employing its EMIB advanced packaging technology. This development marks a significant win for Intel, expanding its foundry customer base which already includes Tesla. While Google's current TPU8i and TPU8t chips utilize TSMC's CoWoS technology, the shift to Intel for the TPUv8e underscores Intel's growing presence in the AI chip manufacturing landscape, with EMIB offering a potentially more versatile and cost-effective solution.

Tesla: AI And Robotics Shift Is The Real Story (NASDAQ:TSLA). Tesla reported strong Q1'26 results, featuring improved gross margins and significant free cash flow growth, despite a revenue miss. Alibaba-Backed DeepRoute.ai Says More Than 300,000 Cars Now Use Its Assisted-Driving System Across Chinese Roads. Alibaba-backed DeepRoute.ai announced that over 300,000 cars in China are now using its advanced assisted-driving system, with a projected increase of 1 million more vehicles this year. The company is focusing on integrating high-end driving features into mass-market vehicles and has shifted to a large-model autonomous-driving strategy. This expansion aligns with China's broader "AI Plus" initiative to embed AI into various industries, including automotive, amidst growing competition from other Chinese automakers and the push by Tesla for full regulatory approval of its FSD in China.

Analyst Questions Why Tesla's 90% Value Hasn't Evaporated Following Elon Musk's FSD Admission: 'It's Astonishing…'. GLJ Research founder Gordon Johnson criticized Tesla's Full Self-Driving (FSD) progress and questioned the company's valuation after Elon Musk admitted that HW3 chips wouldn't support Unsupervised FSD. Johnson believes 80-90% of Tesla's valuation is tied to FSD, and its lack of readiness should have significantly impacted the stock. Despite these concerns, Tesla reported a 16% year-over-year revenue increase and beat analyst earnings estimates for the first quarter of 2026.

Dan Ives: Tesla Is ‘Morphing into a Physical AI Stalwart’ So Don’t Sweat the CapEx and Just Buy TSLA Stock. Wedbush analyst Dan Ives maintains an "Outperform" rating and a Street-high $600 price target on Tesla, believing its significant CapEx is essential for the company's transformation into a "physical AI stalwart." Despite recent stock underperformance due to concerns about margins, demand, and valuation, Tesla reported a Q1 revenue increase of 16% year-over-year and a 52% rise in non-GAAP EPS. While analysts have mixed feelings overall, the consensus price target suggests a 7.82% upside, with Ives' target indicating a 59.5% upside from current levels as the company invests heavily in AI, robotics, and autonomous technology.

Why Tesla's Latest Earnings Don't Justify Its Massive Valuation. Tesla CEO Elon Musk is prioritizing long-term growth through increased capital expenditures, but analysts like Wells Fargo's Colin Langan argue that recent earnings do not justify the company's high valuation. Langan points to significant one-time items boosting earnings and a lack of near-term product catalysts and raised concerns about the execution risk of Tesla's "vision-only" autonomous driving approach. Tesla shares were up 0.53% at $375.70 in premarket trading.

EXCLUSIVE: Ross Gerber Says He's A 'Tesla Lover, Elon Critic' – Why He Sees Himself As The Tesla Hero And Not A Villain. Ross Gerber, CEO of Gerber Kawasaki, describes himself as a "Tesla lover, Elon critic," believing he acts as a hero for Tesla by advocating for the company while criticizing Elon Musk's political narratives. Gerber emphasizes that Tesla is a public company and Musk's personal views should not dominate its brand, arguing that these divisive opinions harm the company's image and sales potential. He highlights his past support for Tesla and Musk, and his current concern that Musk's leadership is negatively impacting the brand, urging a separation of Musk's personal opinions from Tesla's public image.

Target mandates relocation to Minneapolis for 150 remote workers. Target is requiring approximately 150 remote workers, primarily in technology roles, to relocate to its Minneapolis headquarters. This move is part of the company's broader strategy to have most of its headquarters employees working in hybrid roles from its main offices. The affected employees have until July to make the move, with some financial assistance provided by Target.

Target Orders Relocation to HQ for Merchandising Teams. Target Corp. is mandating the relocation of approximately 150 remote merchandising team employees to its Minneapolis headquarters. This move is part of the company's efforts to enhance in-person collaboration, sharpen its focus on style, and accelerate work under the new CEO, Michael Fiddelke. Employees can either accept relocation assistance or leave the company with separation benefits.

CORRECTED-UPDATE 4-Lululemon sinks after new CEO hire from Nike fails to impress investors. Lululemon's shares tumbled about 12% after the company appointed Heidi O'Neill, a former Nike executive, as its new CEO. Investors and analysts are cautious given O'Neill's tenure at Nike coincided with several challenges that Lululemon is currently facing, including slowing demand and market share losses. Founder Chip Wilson and activist investor Elliott Investment Management had different preferences for the CEO role, further complicating the company's turnaround efforts.

UPDATE 2-Lululemon sinks after new CEO hire from Nike fails to impress investors. Lululemon's shares dropped significantly after the announcement of Heidi O'Neill, a former Nike executive, as its new CEO. Investors and analysts expressed caution due to O'Neill's long tenure at Nike during a period when the brand faced challenges similar to Lululemon's current struggles. The appointment also comes amidst pressure from an activist investor and the company's founder, who had pushed for a different candidate.

UPDATE 4-Lululemon sinks after new CEO hire from Nike fails to impress investors. Lululemon shares dropped significantly after the appointment of Heidi O'Neill, formerly of Nike, as its new CEO failed to impress investors. Analysts and investors voiced concerns due to O'Neill's tenure at Nike coinciding with various brand challenges that mirror Lululemon's current struggles. The market had reportedly preferred a turnaround CEO like Jane Nielsen, leading to further investor caution amidst an ongoing proxy fight initiated by founder Chip Wilson.

UPDATE 3-Lululemon sinks after new CEO hire from Nike fails to impress investors. Lululemon Athletica's shares dropped 12% following the announcement of Heidi O'Neill, formerly of Nike, as its new CEO, failing to reassure investors amid struggles including market share losses and activist pressure. Analysts and investors are cautious due to O'Neill's long tenure at Nike, a brand facing similar challenges. Founder Chip Wilson and Elliott Investment Management both expressed concerns and had alternative preferences for the CEO position, contributing to investor unease.
